coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric determination of sarcosine in human urine with enzyme-like reaction mediated Au nanorods etching
coredata.dc:creator: Zhong, Qingmei
Qin, Xiu
Yuan, Chunling
Shi, Rui
Wang, Yilin
coredata.dc:description: A colorimetric method for sensitive detection of sarcosine was established based on enzyme-like reaction mediated etching of Au nanorods (AuNRs). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) originated from litchi peel showed peroxidase-like activity, enabling to accelerate the etching of AuNRs in the presence of H2O2 and I− ions. Sarcosine oxidase (SOX) catalyzed the oxidation of sarcosine to release H2O2 which oxidized I− to I2 under the catalysis of CQDs. AuNRs were then etched by I2 along the longitudinal direction, resulting in a blue-shift of the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) peak of AuNRs. The peak-shift was used for the determination of sarcosine. The influences of pH, CQDs concentration and etching time on the signal response were investigated. Under the optimized conditions, the LSPR peak shift of AuNRs was linearly dependent on sarcosine concentration ranged from 0.02 to 1.0 μmol/L, and the detection limit (3σ/k) was calculated to be 0.011 µmol/L. In order to verify its feasibility and reliability, the method was used for determining sarcosine in human urine samples from healthy volunteers with recoveries ranged from 95.7% to 102.2%, indicating the potential application of the developed method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanorods
Carbon quantum dots
Colorimetry
Sarcosine
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of AuNRs based on seed-mediated growth
Microstructure and peroxidase-like activity characterization of CQDs
CQDs- catalysis and H2O2-mediated etching of AuNRs
Feasibility of the AuNRs-etching based sarcosine detection
Sensitivity for the detection of sarcosine
Selectivity for the determination of sarcosine
Analysis of sarcosine in human urine samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Funding for this work was from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 61664002 ) and the Innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education ( YCSW2020016 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Microstructure characterization of CQDs. (A) TEM image (the insert is HRTEM image of one particle), (B) size distribution, (C) FTIR spectra, (D) XPS spectra.
TEM images (A, B, C and D), and UV–vis absorption spectra (E) of AuNRs incubated with different amounts of sarcosine (a) 0.0, (b) 0.10, (c) 5.00, and (d) 50.0 μmol/L.
UV-NIR spectra of AuNRs (60 μL) in the presence of SOX (0.22 mg/mL), CQDs (4.0 μg/mL), KI (2.0 mmol/L), and various concentrations of sarcosine (0.0 to 10.0 μmol/L).
Selectivity of this method for sarcosine detection. The concentration of sarcosine is 0.1 μmol/L, the other interferences concentrations are 10 μmol/L, respectively.
The schematic illustration the mechanism of the AuNRs-etching based sarcosine detection.
Comparison of different methods for sarcosine detection.
The analysis results of sarcosine in urine samples (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Colorimetric determination of sarcosine in human urine with enzyme-like reaction mediated Au nanorods etching

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Materials, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Materials, Nanning 530004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A colorimetric method for sensitive detection of sarcosine was established based on enzyme-like reaction mediated etching of Au nanorods (AuNRs). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) originated from litchi peel showed peroxidase-like activity, enabling to accelerate the etching of AuNRs in the presence of H2O2 and I− ions. Sarcosine oxidase (SOX) catalyzed the oxidation of sarcosine to release H2O2 which oxidized I− to I2 under the catalysis of CQDs. AuNRs were then etched by I2 along the longitudinal direction, resulting in a blue-shift of the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) peak of AuNRs. The peak-shift was used for the determination of sarcosine. The influences of pH, CQDs concentration and etching time on the signal response were investigated. Under the optimized conditions, the LSPR peak shift of AuNRs was linearly dependent on sarcosine concentration ranged from 0.02 to 1.0 μmol/L, and the detection limit (3σ/k) was calculated to be 0.011 µmol/L. In order to verify its feasibility and reliability, the method was used for determining sarcosine in human urine samples from healthy volunteers with recoveries ranged from 95.7% to 102.2%, indicating the potential application of the developed method.

ce:section.ce:para: Prostate cancer is very common in men, and the early symptoms of prostate cancer are not obvious. Once the symptoms such as hematuria appear, prostate cancer has developed to its advanced stage [1–5]. Therefore, identifying prostate cancer in its early stages is of great significance, which helps reduce the mortality in prostate cancer patients. At present, clinicians mainly rely on the prostate specific antigen (PSA) index to determine whether patients with prostate cancer, but this index is not very reliable. There are many physiological and pathological factors influencing the expression of PSA [6,7]. Even though the method of digital rectal examination combined with PSA examination is used, it cannot accurately distinguish whether the prostatic tumor is malignant or benign, leading some patients with malignant prostate cancer to miss the best treatment period [8]. Hence, more and more researchers begin to question PSA as a biomarker of prostate cancer. Therefore, seeking for new biomarkers that can quickly identify prostate cancer is of great significance for the early diagnosing of prostate cancer. In 2009, it was found that sarcosine was a valuable biomarker for the diagnosis of prostate cancer. The studies confirmed that urinary sarcosine levels in prostate cancer patients could be specifically elevated [4,9]. Up to now, various methods such as fluorescence [10], colorimetry[11,12], HPLC/MS[13], and electrochemistry[14–16] have been available for the determination of sarcosine in urine samples. Each method has its own advantages and disadvantages. Colorimetric analysis mainly depends on the color change of the reaction between the substrate and the analyte or catalyst, which can be read out by bare eyes and measured by UV–vis spectroscopy. Therefore, colorimetric method has received considerable attention in recent years [17–19]. The colorimetric detection of sarcosine is an indirect method with H2O2 as the intermediate. In the presence of sarcosine oxidase (SOX), sarcosine is oxidized to produce H2O2. Carrilho et al. established a colorimetric method for the determination of sarcosine based on the oxidation of 2, 2′- azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium (ABTS) by H2O2 using horseradish peroxidase (HRP) as catalytic agent [8]. Chen′s group constructed a colorimetric sensor for determining sarcosine using Pd nanoparticles to catalyze the H2O2-mediated oxidation of 3, 3′, 5, 5′ - tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) [12]. Recent research found that the folate functionalized polyoxometalate (FA-PMo4V8) could catalyze sarcosine oxidation to releasing H2O2 and H2O2-mediated TMB oxidation. With FA-PMo4V8 acting as mimics of SOX and HRP, a colorimetric method for sensitive detection of sarcosine was established by Wang′s team [20].

Au nanorods (AuNRs) are one-dimensional nanomaterials, and the UV–Vis spectrum of AuNRs often displays two absorption peaks. The weak peak at 520 nm is generally assigned to transverse surface plasmon resonance (TSPR) absorption, whereas the strong peak within visible or near infrared region is due to longitudinal surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) absorption. With a slight change in the longitudinal length of AuNRs, the LSPR peak shifts significantly, which made them widely be used in colorimetric analysis [21–25]. For example, under the catalysis of MoO4 2−, AuNRs can be etched by H2O2 released from glucose oxidase (GOX)-catalyzed glucose oxidation. Based on this, a simple method for colorimetric detection of urine glucose was established by Chen et al [25]. In the presence of acetylcholinesterase (AChE), acetylcholine (ACh) is easily hydrolyzed to choline which can be oxidized to generate H2O2 under the catalysis of choline oxidase. AuNRs are etched by H2O2 under the catalysis of Fe2+ . However, in the presence of organophosphorus pesticides (OPs), AChE is partially deactivated, which reduces the amount of available H2O2, and inhibits the etching of AuNRs. As a result, a simple method for OPs colorimetric determination was constructed based on Fe2+-catalysis and H2O2-mediated etching of AuNRs[26].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a kind of nontoxic nanomaterials, which have been widely used as fluorescent probes [27–30], fluorescent imaging indicators [31,32], photo-catalysts [33–34] and peroxidase mimetics [35,36]. Compared with natural enzymes and other artificial enzymes, CQDs are superior in terms of stability, economic, water solubility and biocompatibility [37–38]. In present study, CQDs originated from discarded litchi peel exhibited peroxidase-like activity, which accelerated the etching of AuNRs in the presence of H2O2 and I−. We herein established a colorimetric method for the detection of sarcosine based on the SOX-catalyzed sarcosine oxidation and CQDs-catalyzed etching of AuNRs. Scheme 1 outlines the mechanism of the AuNRs-etching based sarcosine detection. In the presence of SOX, sarcosine is oxidized to produce H2O2 which immediately oxidizes I− to I2 under the catalysis of CQDs. AuNRs are then etched by I2 along the longitudinal direction, resulting in a blue shift of the LSPR peak of AuNRs. The blue shift of LSPR peak increases with the increase of sarcosine concentration. Thus, the concentration of sarcosine can be determined by the observed shift of LSPR peak.

All materials were purchased from commercial suppliers, doubly deionized water (DDW) was utilized for preparation of all aqueous solutions. Silver nitrate (AgNO3), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), hydroquinone, sarcosine, alanine, cysteine, glycine, histidine, phenylalanine and threonine were supplied by Sinopharm Chemicals Reagent Co. Ltd. (China). Sarcosine oxidase (SOX) from Bacillus sp was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) was obtained from Adamas Reagent (Shanghai, Co., Ltd., China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), potassium iodide (KI), acetic acid (HAc), sodium acetate (NaAc), glucose, urea acid, urea and ascorbic acid were acquired from Damao Chemical Reagent (Tianjin, China).

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analyses were performed on a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN electron microscope (FEI Company, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of CQDs was performed with an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Co., Ltd. USA). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was obtained on a Nicolet iS50 spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Co., Ltd. Madison, WI, USA). The absorption spectra of AuNRs were recorded on a UV-4802 spectrophotometer (Unico, Shanghai, China) with a 1.0 cm quartz cuvette.
The CQDs with peroxidase-like activity were synthesized according to our previous publication [39] with some modifications, and the procedure was provided in supplementary information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of AuNRs based on seed-mediated growth
ce:section.ce:para: The used AuNRs were synthesized by hydroquinone reduction of chloroauric acid [40] (see the detail in supplementary information).

100 μL sarcosine with different concentrations and 50 μL SOX (2.20 mg/mL) were mixed in 2.0 mL PCR tube containing 190 µL HAc–NaAc buffer (0.2 mol/L, pH 4.0), and then incubated at 37 °C for 30 min to produce H2O2. Next, 50 µL CQDs (40 μg/mL), 50 µL KI (20 mM) and 60 µL AuNRs were dropped into the above solution. After this, the solution was incubated at room temperature for 10 min and then subjected to record the absorption spectra ranging from 400 to 1100 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure and peroxidase-like activity characterization of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained CQDs were analysed using TEM to ascertain the dimensions of the particles, XPS and FTIR spectroscopy to determine their functional groups. TEM images showed round shaped morphology characteristic for CQDs (Fig. 1 A), a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (inset in Fig. 1A) corresponding to (101) facet of graphite indicated that the CQDs contain graphite-like structure [41]. The statistics revealed the particle size distribution of CQDs between 2.1 nm and 4.5 nm with average diameter about 3.1 nm (Fig. 1B).

The FTIR spectrum in Fig. 1C discloses that the as-prepared CQDs exhibit a variety of oxygen-containing functional groups on their surface. The characteristic peak at 3434 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of N–H and O–H bonds, the peak at 1379 cm−1 is attributed to in-plane bending vibration of –OH. The carbonyl vibrations can be assigned to stretches at 1621 cm−1, and C–O and C–O–C vibrations may be assigned to the stretch at 1050 cm−1. The existence of these oxygen functional groups (hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxyl) ensures the excellent water solubility of CQDs. XPS was employed to further identify the surface states and elements of the resultant CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1D, the obtained CQDs were mainly composed of three elements including C, N and O with binding energy of 292, 399 and 531 eV, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. S1A) showed three peaks at 284.2, 285.6, 287.4, 292.2 and 295.0 eV, which were ascribed to C=C/C–C, C–N, C–O, C=O and O–C=O bonds, respectively [42]. The spectrum of N 1 s (Fig. S1B) displayed two peaks at 399.3 and 400.9 eV, which were assigned to C–N–C and N–H groups [43]. The spectrum of O 1 s (Fig. S1C) consisted of two peaks at 531.1 and 532.3 eV, which indicated the existence of C=O and C–O–C/C–OH groups [43]. In a word, the FTIR results were in good agreement with XPS data, which confirmed the existence of –COOH, –OH, and –NH2 groups on the surface of CQDs, and ensured the good hydrophilicity of CQDs.

The research disclosed that the peroxidase-like activity of CQDs was due to the existence of carbonyl-based graphite carbon [35]. In this work, such a structure within CQDs was revealed by the HRTEM and FTIR results. To characterize the peroxidase-like activity of the obtained CQDs, control experiments were performed using KI in HAc–NaAc buffer containing either H2O2 only or H2O2 with CQDs. As shown in Fig. S2, KI solution is colorless (inset a) with no absorption in UV–vis spectra (curve a). KI containing H2O2 in the absence of CQDs is almost colorless (inset b) with a very low absorbance at 375 nm (curve b), suggesting that H2O2 can slowly oxidize I− to I2. However, KI involving H2O2 and CQDs form a light brown solution (inset c) with a strong absorption peak at 375 nm (curve c). It turns blue (inset d) immediately after the addition of 0.1% starch, the absorption peak at 375 nm decreases, and a strong absorption peak emerges at 580 nm (curve d). These results confirm that the prepared CQDs display peroxidase-like activity which can catalyze oxidation of I− to I2 in the presence of H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs- catalysis and H2O2-mediated etching of AuNRs
ce:section.ce:para:
The researches [44,45] demonstrated that AuNRs could be etched slowly by H2O2 along with a LSPR peak blue-shift. However, high concentrations of H2O2, strong acid condition and low etching rate limit its applications in biological detection. Since the oxidation ability of hydroxyl radical (·OH) is stronger than that of H2O2, the introduction of Fenton reagent and horseradish peroxidase (HRP) can significantly promote the etching of AuNRs by H2O2. And the colorimetric methods based on enzymatic etching of AuNRs had been applied to the highly sensitive detection of glucose in blood [17]. Besides, it was reported that halide ions (Br−, I−) could accelerate the etching of AuNRs in the presence of H2O2 because they increased the solubility of Au+ ions by the formation of complexes (AuBr2 −, AuI2 −) [44] . A similar phenomenon was observed when CQDs were used as peroxidase mimic in present work. As can be seen in Fig. S3, H2O2 has no obvious effect on the absorption spectra of AuNRs (curve a, b). After the addition of I−, H2O2 in the solution oxidizes I− to I2, which leads to the etching of AuNRs along the longitudinal direction and a blue shift (94 nm) of the LSPR peak (curve c). The existence of CQDs accelerates the oxidation of I − to I2 by H2O2, resulting in a large blue shift (201 nm) of LSPR peak of AuNRs (curve d). We attribute this to the CQDs-catalyzed oxidation of I− to I2 which etches AuNRs more quickly. According to above results and the relevant literatures [46,47], the roles of CQDs and I− can be summarized as follows: (1) under the catalysis of CQDs, I− is oxidized by H2O2 to form I2, (2) the generated I2 and excess I− further form I3 –, and (3) I3 – then oxidizes Au into Au+. The possible chemical reactions can be described as follows:
2 I
-
+ 2 H
+
+ H
2
O
2
→
CQDs
I
2
+ H
2
O
I
2
+ I
-
→
I
3
-
2 A u + I
3
-
+ I
-
→
2 A u I
2
-

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of the AuNRs-etching based sarcosine detection
ce:section.ce:para: Control experiments were performed to investigate the feasibility of the method. TEM images were used to analyze the length of AuNRs before and after etching. As shown in Fig. 2 , the length of AuNRs is initially about 50 nm (Fig. 2A). As the increase of sarcosine concentrations, the AuNRs length gradually decreases (Fig. 2B and C), demonstrating the successful etching of AuNRs. Furthermore, in the presence of sufficient sarcosine, the rod-like shape disappears and spherical particles are obtained (Fig. 2D). The UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 2E) were also used to characterize the etching of AuNRs. Obviously, upon increasing the concentration of sarcosine, the LSPR peak blue shifts and the intensity decreases gradually (curve a, b, and c), and then merges with the TSPR peak at 520 nm (curve d). The merging of the two peaks indicates that the AuNRs have been transformed into gold nanoparticles, which is consistent with the TEM result. It is worth noting that all these results confirm the feasibility of the sensing mechanism for sarcosine detection.

In order to obtain the best analytical performance, the effects of pH, CQDs concentration and etching time on the determination of sarcosine were investigated. The LSPR blue-shift wavelength, defined as ΔW = W 0 – W, is used as optimization criterion, where W 0 and W denote the maximum absorption wavelength of AuNRs in the absence and presence of sarcosine, respectively. As a peroxidase mimetic enzyme, CQDs usually catalyze in weak acid medium. Due to the disproportionation of I2 in alkaline condition, I2-mediated etching of AuNRs is also performed in acidic media. Accordingly, the LSPR peak shift of AuNRs in the HAc–NaAc buffer at pH 2.5 ~ 5.5 was examined. From Fig. S4, it can be observed that the value of ΔW reaches a plateau and remains almost unchanged in the pH range of 3.5 to 4.5. Therefore, pH 4.0 was conservatively chosen for the following studies. The ΔW reaches the maximum when the CQDs concentration is 4.0 μg/mL. the value of ΔW decreases beyond this concentration (Fig. S5). As a result, 4.0 μg/mL was selected as the best CQDs concentration. As shown in Fig. S6, under the catalysis of CQDs, H2O2 generated by SOX-catalyzed sarcosine oxidation rapidly oxidizes I− to I2, and the AuNRs are etched by I2 in 6 min. Considering that longer etching time did not lead to a significant increase in ΔW, 10 min was adopted in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity for the detection of sarcosine
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of sarcosine was further explored under the optimized conditions at various sarcosine concentrations. As shown in Fig. 3 , as the concentration of sarcosine increases from 0.0 to 10.0 μmol/L, the LSPR absorption peak of AuNRs shifts blue and the intensity decreases gradually, which then becomes a shoulder of the TSPR peak. The relationship between sarcosine concentration and the blue-shift wavelength (ΔW) is shown in Fig. S7A, the ΔW is linearly dependent on the sarcosine concentration in the range of 0.02–1.0 μmol/L with a regression equation ΔW = 103.9C + 20.61 and R 2 = 0.993 (Fig. 7B). The limit of detection (LOD) is as low as 0.011 μmol/L (3σ/k, n = 10), showing high sensitivity for sarcosine assay.

A comparison of the present method with previously reported methods for sarcosine determination is summarized in Table 1 and indicates that this method is superior or comparable to most of sarcosine analysis methods in linear range and detection limit. Although the LOD of our method is not the lowest, CQDs, one of the materials used in this study, exhibits several advantages such as easy preparation, low cost and good biocompatibility. Moreover, this method used wavelength shift instead of absorbance for quantitative analysis, which overcome the limitation of deviation from beer's law due to too low or too high absorbance value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity for the determination of sarcosine
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the selectivity of this method in the determination of sarcosine, the most common coexisting substances such as ascorbic acid, urea acid, urea, glucose, and a series of amino acids (alanine, cysteine, glycine, histidine, phenylalanine and threonine), which may interfere with sarcosine detection in human urine, were investigated. The experiments were carried out according to the procedure described in Section 2.3, the concentration of these interferences was 10 μmol/L and that of sarcosine was 0.1 μmol/L. As expected, only sarcosine induced a significant shift of LSPR peak (Fig. 4 ). Because of the good specificity of SOX, the interfering substances could not be oxidized to produce H2O2, I− was not oxidized to I2 and the AuNRs could not be etched. These results suggested that the method developed herein showed superior selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of sarcosine in human urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed method was applied for determining sarcosine in human urine to verify its feasibility. Morning urine samples were provided by three healthy volunteers. The samples were centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 20 min, and the supernatant was diluted with DDW (volume ratio 1:1). Then, 100 μL of the diluted sample was taken for sarcosine detection. The measured concentrations of sarcosine in the original samples were calculated according to calibration curve and dilution ratio. As displayed in Table 2 , the content of sarcosine in three urine samples were 2.46, 2.00, and 2.21 μmol/L, respectively, which were consistent with the range of 0.28 ~ 3.63 μmol/L in healthy people [50]. On the other hand, the recoveries of sarcosine in samples ranged from 95.7% to 102.2% with relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 4.2%, indicating that the proposed method had acceptable reliability and good repeatability in practical sample analysis.

Using UV–vis spectroscopy, we developed a convenient method for sensitive determination of sarcosine based on the SOX-catalyzed sarcosine oxidation, CQDs-catalyzed and H2O2-mediated AuNRs etching. The blue shift of LSPR absorption peak caused by AuNRs etching enabled the detection of sarcosine in the range of 0.02–1.0 μmol/L. The proposed method was simple to carry out and exhibited excellent sensitivity with a detection limit of 0.011 μM, which was applied to the detection of sarcosine in urine samples with satisfactory results. Compared with the traditional colorimetric method based on absorbance change, the colorimetric method based on blue shift of wavelength caused by etching of AuNRs could overcome the limitation of deviation from beer's law caused by too low or too high absorbance, and improve the accuracy of the determination results.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Funding for this work was from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (61664002) and the Innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education (YCSW2020016).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106120.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
